title: SHA-256 truncation to 64 bits for EUI-64/IID derivation lacks security documentation
description:
In lora_l2.c:114-115 and ipv6_addr.c:73-74, only the first 8 bytes (64 bits) of SHA-256 are used:
memcpy(eui64, hash, 8);  // lora_l2.c
memcpy(iid, hash, 8);    // ipv6_addr.c

Locations:
- firmware/bridge-zephyr/subsys/lichen/lora_l2.c:114-115
- firmware/bridge-zephyr/subsys/lichen/ipv6_addr.c:73-74

This is a standard approach (matching RFC 7343 ORCHID) and provides 64-bit collision resistance, which is acceptable for identifiers. However, the code lacks a SECURITY comment explaining:
1. Why 64-bit truncation is sufficient (birthday bound ~2^32 operations)
2. That this is NOT suitable for cryptographic authentication
3. Reference to the spec/RFC justifying this choice

Impact: No vulnerability - this is documentation/clarity debt. A future maintainer might incorrectly assume SHA-256 provides 256-bit security for these identifiers.

Recommendation: Add a SECURITY comment at each truncation point explaining the 64-bit security level and referencing spec section 6.2 or RFC 7343.
close_reason: Added SECURITY comments in lora_l2.c and ipv6_addr.c explaining SHA-256 truncation safety for EUI-64/IID derivation
